title: Capital Account Liberalization and Aggregate Productivity: The Role of Firm Capital Allocation

article.introduction:
In the last three decades, manydeveloping countries have opened their capital accounts, lifting legal restrictions imposed on international capital transactions. There is a growing consensus that capital account liberalization leads to higher economic growth (Quinn and Toyoda (2008)). Higher growth can be the result of factor accumulation or higher productivity. Recent cross-country studies show that financial openness affects growth primarily through higher productivity (Bonfiglioli (2008), Bekaert, Harvey, and Lundblad (2011)). In this paper, we use a large cross-country firm-level data set to show that capital account liberalization increases aggregate productivity through a more efficient allocation of capital across firms.
Specifically, we study the episode of capital account liberalization in 10 Eastern European countries between 1996 and 2013. The timing of these events was driven primarily by the process of accession to the European Union (EU).1We start our analysis by showing that capital account liberalization spurs financial development. Using aggregate data, we exploit variation in the timing of the capital account opening events across countries. We show that capital account liberalization is associated with an increase in the ratio of capital inflows to GDP, an increase in the ratio of private bank credit to GDP, and a decrease in the spread between deposit and lending interest rates.2
Next, we find that capital account liberalization is associated with an increase in country-level total factor productivity (TFP). Aggregate TFP can be higher because firms become individually more productive or because factors are allocated more efficiently across firms. Our analysis shows that a more efficient allocation of capital is a key driver of this effect. We argue that, by spurring financial development, capital account liberalization allows financially constrained firms to demand more capital and produce at a more efficient level. This leads to a more efficient allocation of capital across firms, which increases aggregate TFP.
To guide our analysis, we develop a multisector heterogeneous-firm model of misallocation. Firms in the same sector face different costs of capital because some firms (young, small, opaque) have to borrow funds from an intermediary banking sector, while others can borrow directly from households. This results in a misallocation of capital across firms, which can be measured using the dispersion in the return to capital. Capital misallocation is more severe in sectors where firms depend more on external finance, which we model as higher fixed costs. We model capital account liberalization as a policy that allows banks to obtain funding from abroad at lower cost. We then consider the effects of liberalization on various aggregate, sectoral, and firm-level outcomes. Importantly, the model implies that opening the capital account reduces capital misallocation. As banks raise capital from abroad, bank-dependent firms can borrow more and expand toward their efficient scale. This effect is more pronounced in sectors with higher fixed costs.
Guided by our model, we use a large firm-level data set to estimate the effect of capital account liberalization on within-sector dispersion in the return to capital across firms, as measured by the log marginal revenue product of capital. We first show that the dispersion in the return to capital is systematically higher in manufacturing sectors more dependent on external finance.3Taking advantage of the within-country variation in external financial dependence across sectors, we next show that capital account liberalization reduces the dispersion in the return to capital, particularly in sectors more reliant on external finance. Opening the capital account closes 15% of the gap in the dispersion of the return to capital between sectors at the top and bottom quartiles of the external financial dependence distribution. We find that this effect materializes rather quickly and levels off after six years, indicating a permanent reduction in capital misallocation.
Next, we conduct two sets of tests to determine whether, consistent with our argument, our results are driven by a reduction in financial constraints. First, we show that our results are robust to controlling for changes in several institutional factors that could have been triggered by capital account liberalization and could have also improved capital allocation. Second, based on the predictions of our model, we conduct three tests that provide direct evidence regarding the financial constraints channel. First, we show that, after opening the capital account, firms in financially dependent sectors increase their capital and observe a decrease in the return to capital, indicating that those firms become less financially constrained and produce at a more efficient level. Second, in line with young firms typically being bank-dependent, and thus having fewer sources of external finance than older firms, we find that, within financially dependent sectors, the firms that expand the most are the young firms. Third, we project our measure of return to capital onto age and find that the decline in the dispersion in the return to capital is due to a reduction in the dispersion explained by differences in firm age, which captures differences in financial constraints across firms.
Firms in our sample might be constrained not only because they are young, but also because capital might be allocated to politically connected firms. Consistent with this idea, we find suggestive evidence that the effect of capital account liberalization on capital misallocation is stronger in countries with lower levels of initial corruption, where political connections should be less important. In addition, we show that our main sectoral results are robust to using alternative de jure and de facto capital openness measures. Finally, we use our model of misallocation to map the reduced-form estimates into aggregate TFP gains. According to our calculations, capital account liberalization increases aggregate TFP through more efficient firm capital allocation by 10% to 16%.
Overall, our paper makes three contributions. First, we contribute to the literature on financial openness and economic growth. Bekaert, Harvey, and Lundblad (2005) were the first to show that financial liberalization spurs growth. Bonfiglioli (2008) and Bekaert, Harvey, and Lundblad (2011) find that the effect of financial openness on growth works primarily through higher aggregate productivity. Our paper is the first to connect financial openness to aggregate TFP through the efficiency of firm capital allocation.4
Second, we contribute to the literature on financial markets and factor allocation. Wurgler (2000) documents that financially developed countries increase investment more in their growing sectors and decrease investment more in their declining sectors. Gupta and Yuan (2009) and Levchenko, Ranciere, and Thoenig (2009) show that financial liberalization increases output more in financially dependent sectors. We show that financial liberalization also improves the within-sector allocation of capital across firms.5
Finally, our paper contributes to recent work linking financial constraints to misallocation. Gopinath et al. (2015) study the evolution of capital allocation and productivity in South Europe. In their model, financial liberalization reduces capital misallocation, similar to the predictions of our paper, while a reduction in the real interest rate increases capital misallocation. Based on their finding that the dispersion in the return to capital increased in South Europe after the introduction of the euro, the authors argue that the decline in the real interest rate was more important than financial liberalization for understanding the behavior of productivity in those countries. Our paper is also related to Bai, Carvalho, and Phillips (2015), who show that bank deregulation in the United States led to a reallocation of labor toward small firms with higher marginal products of labor, and Varela (2015), who shows that lifting capital controls in Hungary increased aggregate TFP by improving financing terms and encouraging previously financially constrained firms to invest more.
The remainder of the paper is structured as follows. In SectionI., we analyze the effects of capital account liberalization on financial depth and aggregate TFP. In SectionII., we lay out the analytical framework that we use to guide our empirical analysis. SectionIII.reports the main results of the paper on the effects of capital account liberalization on capital misallocation. In SectionIV., we provide evidence that our results are driven by a reduction in financial constraints. In SectionV., we report robustness checks and map our reduced-form estimates into aggregate TFP gains. SectionVI.concludes.
article.I. Aggregate Evidence:
The traditional approach to measuring financial openness is to use the information provided by the IMF's “Annual Report on Exchange Arrangements and Exchange Restrictions” (AREAER), which reports the extent of rules and regulations affecting cross-border financial transactions. In this paper, we use the capital account openness index of Chinn and Ito (2006), which captures both the extent and intensity of capital mobility restrictions. The Chinn and Ito measure allows us to maximize the time span of our sample. In SectionV., we show that our results are robust to using alternative de jure and de facto capital openness measures.
The Chinn and Ito index is based on a set of four binary measures of capital mobility restrictions that come from AREAER: (1) openness of the capital account, (2) openness of the current account, (3) stringency of requirements for the repatriation of export proceeds, and (4) existence of multiple exchange rates. Each of these measures is set equal to one if the given restriction is nonexistent and zero otherwise. The index is then constructed as the first principal component of the four binary variables. The index has a higher value for countries that are more open to cross-border financial transactions and is normalized to take values between zero and one.
In the Eastern European context, the decision to liberalize capital accounts was driven largely by the prospect of joining the European Union (Arvai (2005)). EU candidate countries had to fully liberalize their capital account by the time of EU accession, as the free movement of capital was one of the major principles of the European Union.6Figure1plots the evolution of the capital account openness index for the 10 Eastern European countries during 1996 to 2013, the period over which we have firm-level data. The three Baltic countries (Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania) started the sample period with a nearly fully liberalized capital account. These three countries became EU members in the first wave of accession in 2004. The other three countries that entered the European Union in this wave (the Czech Republic, Hungary, and Poland) took a more cautious attitude toward capital account liberalization. By 2005, the Czech Republic and Hungary had completely opened their capital accounts. The two countries that became EU members in the second wave of accession in 2007 (Bulgaria and Romania) liberalized their capital accounts in the mid-2000s. Russia started opening its capital account toward the end of the 2000s. Ukraine is the only country in the sample that remained closed throughout the sample period.
We start by estimating the effect of capital account liberalization on capital inflows, financial depth, and the banking interest rate spread for the 10 countries in our sample over the period 1996 to 2013. Capital inflows are the sum of foreign direct investment, portfolio investment, and other investment.7We normalize capital inflows by GDP of the recipient economy. Private credit is domestic credit to the private sector by banks, which we normalize by each country's GDP. The banking interest rate spread is the difference between the interest rate charged by banks on loans and the interest rate paid for deposits.8
TableIreports the results. We define a capital account liberalization event as a one-standard-deviation increase in the capital openness index, that is,ΔCapitalOpenness=0.35. According to column (1), capital account liberalization is associated with an increase in the capital inflows-to-GDP ratio of 2.5 percentage points (=0.069∗0.35). Columns (2) and (3) show that capital account liberalization is associated with an increase in the private credit-to-GDP ratio of 8.5 percentage points and a decrease in the interest rate spread of 5.2 percentage points, respectively. These results are consistent with Prediction 2 of the model discussed in the next section. However, the results should be interpreted with caution. Because the timing of the capital account opening events was determined largely by the process of EU accession, it is unlikely that our results are driven by reverse causality. It is possible, however, that the timing of these events coincided with other policies. We therefore view these results as suggestive evidence that capital account liberalization spurred financial development.
Because of the constant-returns-to-scale assumption, we can measure the labor elasticity for each country as the ratio of labor compensation to income, which is available from the PWTs. Capital elasticity is calculated as one minus the labor elasticity. We reestimate equation1using log TFP as the dependent variable. According to column (4) of TableI, capital account liberalization is associated with an increase in aggregate TFP of 12% (=0.337∗0.35), in line with Prediction 2 of the model developed below. To address the possibility that other policies could be taking place at the same time, we control for the standard pro-market policies in transition economies: trade liberalization, privatization, price liberalization, competition, infrastructure, and restructuring.12From column (5), we see that our results are robust to controlling for these other policies.
article.II. Analytical Framework:
In this section, we provide a framework of capital account liberalization and capital misallocation in a multisector model with heterogeneous firms. The model features three types of agents: consumers, firms, and a monopolist bank. Consumers provide labor and capital services inelastically, and consume an aggregate output ofSsectors,Q=∏s=1SQsθs, implying constant expenditure shares:PsQs=θsQ.13A fixed number ofNsfirms produces in each sectors, and goods of different firms are imperfectly substitutable. Firms, denoted byi, are monopolistic competitors with a Cobb-Douglas production function,qsi=zsiksiαslsi1−αs. Sectoral real output isQs=(∑i=1Nsqsiσ−1σ)σσ−1.
There are two types of firms in each sector, denoted byg∈{1,2}, so thatNs=Ns1+Ns2. The two types of firms differ in their access to funding. Type-1 firms are young, small, and opaque, and can access funds only through an intermediary banking sector. Type-2 firms, in contrast, can borrow funds directly from households (through corporate bonds). The banking sector takes deposits from households and lends to type-1 firms in each sector. Because the banking sector is imperfectly competitive, the bank will charge a markup over the deposit rater, and thus the cost of capital of type-1 firms exceeds that for type-2 firms.
In addition, firms may face idiosyncratic frictions that distort their capital and labor choices, such as political connections that make access to capital easier for some firms or firing costs that differ among firms. We denote such frictions by1+τsiKand1+τsiL, respectively, and treat them as exogenously given for our purpose. For simplicity, we assume that both groups of firms in a sector face the same joint distribution of productivity and exogenous frictions, and that the share of type-1 firms is the same across sectors.
Labor supply is exogenous and labor market clearing is given byL=∑s=1S∑i=1Nslsi. If the economy is closed to international capital flows, the deposit interest rate is determined by the capital market equilibrium,A=∑s=1S∑i=1Ns(ksi+fs), and the lending rate in each sector is given through the sector-specific markup rule.
Prediction 1. (Dispersion of Returns)Sectors with higher fixed costs have higher dispersion of the return to capital:

Intuitively, higher fixed costs translate into a larger inelastic component of demand. The bank will exploit this lower elasticity by charging higher markups from firms, thus increasing the disparity in the cost of capital across the two groups of firms. We provide derivations of this prediction and those below in the Internet Appendix.
We model capital account liberalization as a policy that allows the bank to raise deposits from abroad at lower cost, that is, as an exogenous reduction to the deposit rater,Δr<0. In the new equilibrium, the interest rate is given and replaces the equilibrium condition in the capital market. The bank and type-2 firms can freely borrow funds at the lower world interest rate. However, type-1 firms continue borrowing only from the bank.
Prediction 2. (Aggregate Effects)Capital account liberalization increases bank lending, decreases the interest rate spread, and increases aggregate TFP:


The intuition behind this result is that a reduction in the deposit rate implies a lower marginal cost for the bank, which induces the bank to lend more to firms. However, as lending increases and firms can scale up their production, demand for funding from the bank becomes more elastic because the fixed cost become relatively less important. Facing a higher demand elasticity, the bank will then choose a lower markup. The effect on aggregate TFP operates through reduced capital misallocation, as explained next.18
Prediction 3. (Capital Misallocation Effects)Capital account liberalization decreases the dispersion in the return to capital. The effect is stronger in sectors with higher fixed costs:


Intuitively, the dispersion in the return to capital is directly related to the size of the interest rate spread. A reduction in the interest rate spread thus reduces capital misallocation in every sector, but particularly in sectors with higher fixed costs. In contrast, there is no effect on the dispersion in the return to labor. The effect on the covariance between the factor returns is ambiguous.19
Prediction 4. (Firm-Level Effects)Capital account liberalization increases average firm capital more in sectors with higher fixed costs:


Because the decline in the interest rate spread is larger in sectors with higher fixed costs, the average MRPK will fall, as firms in those sectors expand their capital the most. However, the effect is heterogeneous across type-1 and type-2 firms, as explained below.
Prediction 5. (Firm-Level Effects by Type)Capital account liberalization increases the capital of type-1 firms more than that of type-2 firms in the same sector. The differential effect is larger in sectors with higher fixed costs:


The intuition behind this result is that type-1 firms benefit from both the reduction in the deposit interest rate and the reduction in the markup, while type-2 firms benefit only from the interest rate reduction. The differential effect is larger in those sectors in which the markup declines by more, that is, in sectors with higher fixed costs.
article.III. Microevidence:
Our firm-level data come from Amadeus, a commercial data set provided by Bureau van Dijk (BvD). Amadeus contains financial statements on millions of European firms. BvD collects data from local information providers, which in most cases are the local company registers. The vast majority of firms covered by Amadeus are unlisted and correspond to small and medium-sized enterprises. Because financial constraints are particularly binding for smaller and younger firms, this represents a distinct advantage over data sets that only contain listed companies (e.g., Worldscope), which are typically larger and older.
The data set comes in yearly versions and each vintage includes up to 10 years of information per firm. If a firm stops filing, it remains in the data set for four consecutive years and then is dropped. As explained in the Internet Appendix, we overcome this survivorship bias by appending various versions of Amadeus. We follow the literature on productivity and focus exclusively on manufacturing firms. In the Internet Appendix, we explain the step-by-step data-cleaning procedure. After cleaning the data, we are left with roughly 2.2 million observations for 463,000 companies from 1996 to 2013, which is the last year with comprehensive Amadeus coverage.
Table IAI reports firm-level summary statistics for sales, capital stock, and return to capital by country over the period 1996 to 2013. We measure the capital stock of a firm by its fixed assets (i.e., property, plant, and equipment). The first column reports the number of firms for each country. Differences in the number of firms across countries are due to differences in firms' filing requirements. In most cases, those filing requirements are related to size criteria or to the mode of incorporation. The second set of columns reports summary statistics for firm sales for each country. Note that we have data only on sales, not on prices. Thus, we cannot measure physical output and back out physical productivity directly. Finally, the last set of columns reports summary statistics for the return to capital, which is a revenue-based measure of the marginal product of capital.
The calculations for the dispersion in the return to labor and the covariance between the factor returns are analogous. To calculate the dispersion in the return to capital, we do not require estimates of sector-level factor elasticities. However, we will need these estimates in SectionV., when we map the reduced-form estimates into aggregate productivity gains. We follow the approach of Hsieh and Klenow (2009) and measure factor elasticities from sectoral income shares in the United States. We assume that the labor elasticity of each sector (1−αs) is the same across countries and measure this elasticity as the ratio of labor compensation to income.20
TableIIreports sector-level summary statistics for the dispersion in the return to capital, return to labor, and covariance between the factor returns over the period 1996 to 2013. There are 22 two-digit manufacturing sectors in the sample (ISIC Revision 3).21The average dispersion in the return to capital is 1.707, although there is a lot of variation across sectors, ranging from 1.02 (tobacco) to 2.03 (office machinery). The average dispersion in the return to capital is higher than the dispersion in the return to labor, suggesting a larger degree of capital misallocation in the economy relative to labor. The covariance between the factor returns is positive.
To provide a benchmark for these numbers, we calculate the dispersion in factor returns during the same sample period (1996 to 2013) for the five most developed countries in continental Europe: Austria, Belgium, France, Germany, and the Netherlands. Because these countries have more developed financial markets and stronger institutions, we expect factors to be more efficiently allocated across firms. Internet Appendix Table IAII shows that this is indeed the case. The average dispersion in the return to capital in developed Europe is 30% lower than in our Eastern European sample (=1−1.199/1.707). We observe a similar result for the dispersion in the return to labor. This indicates that factor misallocation is substantially less severe in more developed economies, presumably because they suffer fewer constraints and distortions.22
Rajan and Zingales (1998) show that financial constraints are more binding for firms in sectors that are more dependent on external finance. External financial dependence is defined as the fraction of capital expenditures not financed with internal cash flows. For technological reasons, some sectors require more external finance than others because firms operate at a larger scale or have a longer gestation period. Rajan and Zingales (1998) construct a financial dependence index using the median financial dependence across U.S. publicly traded firms in each manufacturing sector.23
The last column of TableIIreports the external financial dependence index for the 22 manufacturing sectors in the sample. Sectors with low financial dependence include tobacco and textiles; sectors with high dependence include machinery and professional equipment. In this paper, we extrapolate the U.S.-based financial dependence measure to our sample of Eastern European countries based on the assumption that sectoral technological differences persist across countries. Note that we do not require each country to have the same value of financial dependence in each sector. Rather, our assumption is that the ranking of financial dependence across sectors is the same in each country.
Figure2shows the relationship between the dispersion in the return to capital and external financial dependence in the cross-section of sectors.24Consistent with Prediction 1 of the model, the figure depicts a strong positive relationship between the two measures: capital misallocation is more severe in sectors that rely more heavily on external finance. In the empirical analysis below, we analyze the effects of capital account liberalization on sectors with different needs for external finance.
When estimating the effect of capital account liberalization on aggregate TFP, our main concern is that other policies could be taking place at the same time: If those other policies similarly affected sectors with different needs for external finance, our cross-sectoral comparison would cancel out their effects. To address the possibility that other policies could differentially affect sectors with different financial dependence, we control for the interaction between the same pro-market policies used in SectionI.and the financial dependence index.
TableIIIreports the results. Column (1) shows that capital account liberalization decreases the dispersion in the return to capital, particularly in sectors with a high need for external finance. This is consistent with Prediction 3 of the model. To understand the magnitude of the effect, consider two sectors: one at the 75thpercentile of the financial dependence index (motor vehicles) and one at the 25thpercentile (dressing of leather). The difference in financial dependence between the two sectors is 0.26 (=0.46−0.2). Capital account liberalization decreases the dispersion in the return to capital in the high-financial-dependence sector by 0.045 more than in the low-financial-dependence sector (=−0.488∗0.35∗0.26). This implies that capital account liberalization closes 15% of the average gap in the dispersion in the return to capital across sectors at the bottom and top quartiles of the external financial dependence distribution.27According to column (2), the effect on the dispersion in the return to labor is not statistically significant, which is also in line with Prediction 3. This implies that the extent of misallocation of labor across firms was unaffected by the changes in capital account policy. Finally, column (3) shows a marginally significant negative effect on the covariance between the factor returns.
article.IV. Inspecting the Mechanism:
In this section, we perform two sets of tests providing evidence consistent with the argument that our results are driven by a reduction in financial constraints.
One challenge that we face is that capital account liberalization might have brought changes to corporate governance and other institutions. For example, Bekaert, Harvey, and Lundblad (2011) show that increased foreign presence after liberalization improves the institutional environment. An institutional improvement may have reduced capital misallocation for reasons unrelated to financial frictions. In the first set of tests, we show that our results are robust to controlling for several such factors.
We focus on the institutional factors analyzed by Bekaert, Harvey, and Lundblad (2011): investment profile, macroeconomic environment, and quality of institutions.29Investment profile is a measure of the government's attitude to inward investment, macroeconomic environment is an economic risk indicator combining different macroeconomic variables (such as inflation and fiscal budget), and quality of institutions is the sum of several of the ICRG's political risk subcomponents, including law and order and bureaucratic quality. Because there was a widespread adoption of international financial reporting standards (IFRS) around our sample period, we also look at the adoption dates of IFRS standards.30
We reestimate equation7controlling for the institutional factors and IFRS adoption dates. We include the different controls individually as well as simultaneously. TableIVreports the results. The effect of capital account liberalization on capital misallocation remains sizable and significant in all specifications. This does not mean that the alternative factors considered do not affect capital misallocation (in fact, all three institutional factors are associated with lower dispersion in the return to capital). Instead, this means that the effects of capital liberalization that we capture are incremental to the effects of these alternative factors. Combined with the firm-level evidence reported below, this evidence suggests that our results are driven by a reduction in financial constraints.
TableVreports the results. Column (1) shows that capital account liberalization reduces the return to capital of firms in sectors with a high need for external finance relative to firms in sectors with a low need for finance. In line with Prediction 4 of the model, this result indicates that firms in those sectors become less constrained and can produce at a more efficient level with their return to capital closer to the cost of capital. Column (2) shows that, after the capital account is opened, firms in sectors with high relative to low financial dependence increase their capital stock by roughly 3.5% (=0.36∗0.35∗0.26). According to column (3), those firms do not increase their employment.
Next, we explore whether the firms that expanded in financially dependent sectors invested in productive projects. If those firms invested in negative NPV projects, they should be more likely to go bankrupt several years after expanding. We start the analysis by looking at the subset of firms that appeared in the data before the capital account was opened. Next, we check whether those firms appear in the data in the last year of the sample (2013). We assume that if a firm disappears from the data, it went bankrupt.31We collapse the data into a country-firm cross-section. Then, we create a dummy equal to one if the firm disappeared from the sample after liberalization and zero otherwise. Finally, we regress this dummy on sectoral external financial dependence and country fixed effects. From Internet Appendix Table IAIII, we observe that the coefficient in this regression is small and not significant. This suggests that, after liberalization, firms that expanded in high-financial-dependence sectors did not go bankrupt more often than firms in low-financial-dependence sectors.
According to Prediction 5 of the model, type-1 firms (bank-dependent firms) benefit more from capital account liberalization. Because young firms are typically bank-dependent, they have fewer sources of external finance than older firms.32In our second test, we use young firms as an empirical counterpart of type-1 firms. Above, we showed that, after capital account liberalization, capital flows toward firms in sectors with high dependence on external finance. Now, we analyze whether, within those sectors, capital flows more to previously constrained, young firms. To analyze how the effect of the policy varies with firm age, we separate our sample into young and old firms by their founding year. The median founding year across all firms in the sample is 1998, so we define young firms as those founded in 1998 and later.
If young firms are more financially constrained than old firms, they should have a higher return to capital. Moreover, the difference in the return to capital between young and old firms should be larger in sectors more dependent on external finance. Figure4shows that this is indeed the case. The figure plots the average difference in the return to capital between young and old firms in each sector against the external financial dependence index. We observe that this difference is greater than zero in every sector. The positive slope means that young firms are more constrained than old firms, particularly in high-financial-dependence sectors.
TableVIreports the results. Column (1) shows that the triple interaction term is positive and significant, indicating that young firms increased their capital stock relative to old firms, particularly in highly financially dependent sectors. This result is in line with Prediction 5 of the model. Capital account liberalization increases the capital stock of young firms by 5% more than old firms, in sectors with high relative to low financial dependence (=0.546∗0.35∗0.26). Column (2) shows that the effect on employment is also positive, but estimated imprecisely.
article.V. Robustness Checks and Aggregation:
Firms in our sample may be constrained not only because they are young, but also because capital may be allocated to politically connected firms. One could construct measures of political connections for the publicly listed firms in our sample.34Because the vast majority of firms in our sample are privately held, we focus instead on a country-level measure of political connections. In particular, we use the corruption measure provided by the World Bank's Enterprise Survey, which is based on face-to-face interviews with managers and owners of small and medium-sized enterprises. The measure is defined as the percentage of firms identifying corruption as a major constraint in each country.
The results of this regression are reported in column (1) of TableVIII. The triple interaction term is positive and large in magnitude. The effect is not estimated precisely (p-value of 0.14), however, due to the fact that we are exploiting variation in corruption across a relatively small sample of countries. That said, the evidence suggests that the effect of capital account liberalization is stronger in countries with lower levels of initial corruption. This result is consistent with Bekaert, Harvey, and Lundblad (2011), who find that countries that have higher quality institutions experience larger productivity gains from financial openness.
Throughout the paper, we have used the Chinn and Ito (2006) capital account openness index as our openness measure. In this section, we show that our results are robust to using alternative openness measures. First, we use the precise opening dates identified in SectionIII.to define a liberalization dummy that is equal to one after the opening date and zero otherwise. Column (2) of TableVIIIreports the results of estimating equation7using this alternative binary liberalization measure. The effect is negative and highly significant.
Second, we use the de jure measure of Abiad, Detragiache, and Tressel (2010). This measure is also based on the IMF's AREAER, although it is coarser than the Chinn and Ito (2006) measure. It is constructed as the sum of the scores for three questions: (1) is the exchange rate system unified, (2) does a country set restrictions on capital inflows, and (3) does a country set restrictions on capital outflows? Column (3) reports the results using this alternative de jure measure.35The results are robust to using this openness measure.
Finally, we use a de facto openness measure, which considers the actual participation of a country in international capital markets. In particular, we use a country's external position as a ratio of GDP. To be consistent with the analysis of SectionI., we define de facto openness as the ratio of foreign total liabilities to GDP.36Column (4) of TableVIIIreports the results using this de facto openness measure. We find that an increase in actual participation in international capital markets leads to a statistically significant reduction in capital misallocation.
We report the results of this calculation in TableIX. The magnitude of the effect depends on two parameters: the elasticity of substitution σ and the sectoral distributionθs=(PsYs)/Y. In the upper panel of TableIX, we calculate the effect for the average initial sectoral distribution (θs) of the 10 countries.38For the benchmark elasticity of substitutionσ=3(as in Hsieh and Klenow (2009)), the magnitude of the effect is 13.1%. When we setσ=2, the effect decreases to 9.7%; when we setσ=4, the effect increases to 16.4%. In the lower panel of TableIX, we calculate the effect for the initial sectoral distributions of the individual countries. The effect on aggregate TFP is stronger for the countries more specialized in sectors with high external financial dependence. For our benchmark calculation, TFP gains range from 10% for Bulgaria to 15.5% for Russia.
Finally, recall that in SectionI.we find that capital account liberalization is associated with an increase in aggregate TFP of 12%. This effect was calculated for the whole economy, including manufacturing and nonmanufacturing sectors. The results of our calibration, in contrast, correspond only to manufacturing sectors. Because manufacturing output in our sample countries accounts for roughly one-third of total output, our calculations suggest that improved firm capital allocation explains between 28% (=10%∗1/3∗1/12%) and 43% (=15.5%∗1/3∗1/12%) of the estimated total aggregate TFP gains.
article.VI. Conclusions:
In this paper, we use a large cross-country firm-level data set to analyze the effects of capital account liberalization on firm capital allocation and aggregate productivity. We focus our analysis on 10 Eastern European countries that lifted restrictions on capital account transactions between 1996 and 2013. We argue that, by reducing financial constraints, capital account liberalization leads to a more efficient allocation of capital across firms and higher aggregate TFP. Our paper is the first to connect financial openness and aggregate TFP through the efficiency of firm capital allocation.
We start the analysis by using aggregate data and estimate the effect of capital account liberalization on aggregate productivity. We exploit the variation in the timing of the capital account opening events across countries and find that capital account liberalization is associated with a significant increase in aggregate TFP. We further show that a key factor behind the aggregate productivity gains is a more efficient allocation of capital across firms. To guide our analysis, we develop a multisector heterogeneous-firm model of misallocation. According to the model, capital is allocated inefficiently because some firms (young, small, opaque) can borrow only from banks and face a relatively high cost of capital. In the model, capital account liberalization allows banks to raise capital from abroad and increase lending. As a result, bank-dependent firms can borrow more and produce closer to their efficient level.
Guided by the model, we use a large firm-level data set and measure capital misallocation as the dispersion in the return to capital across firms. We exploit the within-country variation in external financial dependence across sectors and find that capital account liberalization decreases the dispersion in the return to capital in sectors with high financial dependence more than in sectors with low dependence. Opening the capital account closes 15% of the gap in the dispersion in the return to capital between sectors with high and low financial dependence. We perform a series of tests providing evidence consistent with the argument that our results are driven by a reduction in financial constraints. Finally, we use our model of misallocation to map the reduced-form estimates into aggregate TFP gains. We document that capital account liberalization increases aggregate TFP through more efficient firm capital allocation by 10% to 16%.
Editors: Bruno Biais, Michael R. Roberts, and Kenneth J. Singleton
